content:
# Exodus Chapter 15 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~40r–41r (PDF pp. 77–79); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 55–56 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_015_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_015_page_002.jpg`, `EXO_015_page_003.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_015_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_015_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## The Song of Moses — First Stanza: Exaltation of the LORD (vv. 1–5)

**1** Then Moses and the sons of Israel sang [**yashir**] this song [**hashirah hazot**] to the LORD, and they spoke, saying:

> "I will sing [**ashirah**] to the LORD, for He is highly exalted [**ga'oh ga'ah**];
> the horse and its rider [**sus verokhvo**] He has hurled [**ramah**] into the sea.

**2**
> The LORD is my strength [**azzi**] and my song [**vezimrat**] Yah,
> and He has become my salvation [**lishu'ah**].
> This is my God, and I will glorify Him [**ve'anvehu**],
> the God of my father, and I will exalt Him [**va'aromemenhu**].

**3**
> The LORD is a man of war [**ish milchamah**];
> the LORD is His name [**YHWH shemo**].

**4**
> The chariots of Pharaoh and his army [**vecheilo**]
> He has cast [**yarah**] into the sea,
> and the choice of his officers [**mivchar shalishav**]
> were drowned [**tubba'u**] in the Sea of Reeds [**veYam-Suf**].

**5**
> The deep waters [**tehomot**] covered them;
> they went down into the depths [**metsolot**] like a stone [**kemo-aven**].

## Second Stanza: The Right Hand of the LORD (vv. 6–10)

**6**
> Your right hand [**yeminekha**], O LORD, is majestic [**ne'dari**] in power;
> Your right hand, O LORD, shatters [**tir'ats**] the enemy.

**7**
> And in the greatness of Your majesty [**uverov ge'onekha**]
> You overthrow [**taharos**] those who rise against You.
> You send forth Your burning anger [**charonekha**];
> it consumes them [**yokhlemo**] like stubble [**kaqash**].

**8**
> And by the blast [**ruach**] of Your nostrils [**appekha**]
> the waters were heaped up [**ne'ermu mayim**];
> the flowing waters stood up [**nitsvu**] like a heap [**kemo-ned**];
> the deep waters [**tehomot**] congealed [**qafe'u**] in the heart of the sea.

**9**
> The enemy [**oyev**] said,
> "I will pursue [**erdof**], I will overtake [**assig**],
> I will divide [**achaleq**] the spoil [**shalal**];
> my soul [**nafshi**] shall be filled [**timla'emo**] with them.
> I will draw [**ariq**] my sword [**charbi**];
> my hand [**yadi**] shall dispossess them [**torishemo**]."

**10**
> You blew [**nashafta**] with Your wind [**beruchakha**];
> the sea covered them [**kissamo yam**].
> They sank [**tsalalu**] like lead [**ka'oferet**]
> in the mighty [**addirim**] waters.

## Third Stanza: The Incomparability of the LORD (vv. 11–13)

**11**
> Who is like You [**mi kamokha**] among the gods [**ba'elim**], O LORD?
> Who is like You, majestic [**ne'dar**] in holiness [**baqqodesh**],
> fearful [**nora**] in praises [**tehillot**],
> doing wonders [**oseh fele**]?

**12**
> You stretched out [**natita**] Your right hand [**yeminekha**];
> the earth [**erets**] swallowed them [**tivla'emo**].

**13**
> You have led [**nachita**] in Your steadfast love [**vechasdekha**]
> the people [**am-zu**] whom You have redeemed [**ga'alta**].
> You have guided [**nehalta**] them in Your strength [**be'azzekha**]
> to Your holy dwelling [**el-neveh qodshekha**].

## Fourth Stanza: The Terror of the Nations (vv. 14–16a)

**14**
> The peoples [**ammim**] have heard [**shame'u**]; they tremble [**yirgazun**].
> Anguish [**chil**] has seized [**achaz**] the inhabitants [**yoshvey**] of Philistia.

**15**
> Then the chiefs [**allufey**] of Edom were dismayed [**nivhalu**];
> the leaders [**eyley**] of Moab — trembling [**ra'ad**] seizes them.
> All the inhabitants of Canaan have melted away [**namogu**].

**16a**
> Terror [**ematah**] and dread [**vafachad**] fall upon them.
> By the greatness of Your arm [**bigdol zero'akha**]
> they are still [**yiddemu**] as a stone [**ka'aven**] —

## Fifth Stanza: The LORD Brings His People Home (vv. 16b–18)

**16b**
> until Your people [**ammekha**] pass over [**ya'avor**], O LORD,
> until the people whom You have acquired [**qanita**] pass over.

**17**
> You will bring them [**tevi'emo**] and plant them [**vitta'emo**]
> on the mountain [**behar**] of Your inheritance [**nachalatekha**],
> the place [**makhon**] You have made for Your dwelling [**leshivtekha**], O LORD,
> the sanctuary [**miqdash**], O Lord [**Adonai**],
> which Your hands [**yadekha**] have established [**konenu**].

**18**
> The LORD will reign [**yimlokh**]
> forever and ever [**le'olam va'ed**]."

## Narrative Resumption: The Sea Closes Over Egypt (v. 19)

**19** For the horse of Pharaoh, with his chariots and with his horsemen, went into the sea, and the LORD brought back [**vayyashev**] the waters of the sea upon them. And the sons of Israel walked on the dry ground [**bayyabbashah**] in the midst of the sea.

## Miriam's Song (vv. 20–21)

**20** And Miriam the prophetess [**hannevi'ah**], the sister of Aaron, took [**vattiqqach**] the timbrel [**hatof**] in her hand, and all the women went out [**vattetse'na**] after her with timbrels and with dances [**uvimcholot**].

**21** And Miriam answered [**vatta'an**] them:

> "Sing [**shiru**] to the LORD, for He is highly exalted [**ga'oh ga'ah**];
> the horse and its rider He has hurled [**ramah**] into the sea."

## The Waters of Marah (vv. 22–25)

**22** And Moses caused Israel to journey [**vayyassa**] from the Sea of Reeds, and they went out into the wilderness of Shur [**midbar-Shur**]. And they went three days [**sheloshet yamim**] in the wilderness and did not find water [**mayim**].

**23** And they came to Marah [**Marah**], and they were not able [**lo yakhlu**] to drink the waters from Marah, for they were bitter [**marim hem**]. Therefore its name was called [**qara shemah**] Marah.

**24** And the people murmured [**vayyillonu**] against Moses, saying, "What shall we drink [**mah-nishteh**]?"

**25** And he cried out [**vayyits'aq**] to the LORD, and the LORD showed him [**vayyorehu**] a tree [**ets**]. And he cast [**vayyashleikh**] it into the waters, and the waters became sweet [**vayyimtequ hammayim**]. There He made [**sam**] for them a statute [**choq**] and an ordinance [**umishpat**], and there He tested them [**nissahu**].

## The LORD Who Heals (vv. 26–27)

**26** And He said, "If you will diligently listen [**shamo'a tishma**] to the voice of the LORD your God, and do what is right [**hayyashar**] in His eyes, and give ear [**veha'azanta**] to His commandments [**lemitsvotav**], and keep [**veshamarta**] all His statutes [**kol-chuqqav**] — every disease [**kol-hammachalah**] which I have put [**samti**] upon Egypt, I will not put [**lo-asim**] upon you, for I am the LORD your healer [**rofekha**]."

**27** And they came to Elim [**Elim**], and there were twelve [**shtem esreh**] springs of water [**enot mayim**] and seventy [**shiv'im**] date palms [**temarim**]. And they encamped [**vayyachanu**] there by the waters [**al-hammayim**].

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations
The Leningrad Codex folios ~40r–41r (PDF pp. 77–79) contain the entirety of Exodus 15. The most visually distinctive feature of these folios is the special scribal layout of the Song of the Sea (Shirat HaYam, vv. 1b–18), which is written in the traditional "brick-over-half-brick" pattern (ariach al gabey leveinah) visible on pages 78–79 (the second and third images). This is one of only two passages in the Torah written in this shirah layout — the other being the Song of Moses in Deuteronomy 32. In this layout, each line of text is divided into segments with blank spaces alternating in position from line to line, so that a space on one line sits above text on the next line and vice versa, resembling the staggered pattern of bricks in a wall. This special layout is prescribed by the Talmud (Megillah 16b; Soferim 12:10–11) and marks the Song as a passage of unique liturgical and theological importance. The scribal tradition holds that this layout reflects the crossing itself — the alternating text and space representing the walls of water with the path between them. The beginning of the Song (v. 1b, az yashir Mosheh) is preceded by a clear division mark, and the narrative resumption at v. 19 (ki ba sus Par'oh) returns to the standard three-column prose layout.

A petuchah (open paragraph) marker appears before 15:1, signaling that the Song of the Sea opens a major new literary unit following the prose account of the crossing in chapter 14. Within the Song itself, the shirah layout functions as the paragraph division — the visual structure is the division. A setumah (closed paragraph) marker appears before v. 19, where the narrative voice resumes with a prose summary of the Sea event. Another setumah appears before v. 20, introducing Miriam's antiphonal response. A petuchah marker appears before v. 22, marking the transition from the Sea of Reeds celebration to the wilderness journey — a major thematic shift from triumph to testing. A setumah appears before v. 27, separating the Marah episode from the arrival at Elim.

The masorah parva annotations are visible throughout. Annotations appear on the phrase ga'oh ga'ah (v. 1, "highly exalted" — the cognate accusative construction), on zimrat Yah (v. 2, "song of Yah" — noting the abbreviated divine name), on ish milchamah (v. 3, "man of war" — the anthropomorphic metaphor), on mi kamokha (v. 11, "who is like You" — marking this as a liturgically significant phrase), on nachalatekha (v. 17, "Your inheritance" — noting its connection to the conquest theme), on hannevi'ah (v. 20, "the prophetess" — the first woman designated as a prophetess in the Torah), and on rofekha (v. 26, "your healer" — marking the divine epithet). The cantillation on v. 1b places the etnachta under laYHWH ("to the LORD"), creating a dramatic pause before the content of the Song. The cantillation on v. 11 gives special emphasis to mi kamokha ba'elim YHWH ("Who is like You among the gods, O LORD"), with the distinctive trope pattern used for this liturgically central line. The cantillation on v. 18 marks YHWH yimlokh le'olam va'ed ("The LORD will reign forever and ever") with the climactic cadence that closes the Song. The cantillation on v. 26 places emphasis on ki ani YHWH rofekha ("for I am the LORD your healer"), marking the self-revelatory divine speech.

The Codex Vaticanus pages 55–56 preserve the LXX text of Exodus 15. The Song begins on page 55 (continuing from chapter 14) and extends onto page 56. The Vaticanus text of the Song follows the MT structure closely. At v. 1, the LXX renders ga'oh ga'ah as endoxōs gar dedoxastai ("for He has been gloriously glorified"), using the root doxazō ("to glorify") to translate the Hebrew ga'ah ("to be exalted, to rise up") — a significant interpretive shift from "exaltation" to "glorification." At v. 2, the LXX renders azzi vezimrat Yah as boēthos kai skepastēs egeneto moi eis sōtērian ("helper and protector He became to me for salvation"), diverging notably from the MT; the problematic zimrat is not rendered as "song" but as "protector" (skepastēs), and the abbreviated divine name Yah is not reproduced. At v. 3, the LXX renders ish milchamah as suntribōn polemous ("one who crushes wars"), avoiding the anthropomorphic "man of war" and replacing it with a participial phrase. The divine name YHWH shemo ("the LORD is His name") is rendered kurios onoma autō ("the Lord is His name"). At v. 8, the LXX renders ne'ermu mayim as epagē hōsei teichos ta hudata ("the waters were frozen like a wall"), using pēgnumi ("to fix, to freeze, to congeal"), and kemo-ned as hōsei teichos ("like a wall"), interpreting ned ("heap") as teichos ("wall"). At v. 11, the LXX renders mi kamokha ba'elim as tis homoios soi en theois kurie ("who is like You among the gods, O Lord"), rendering ba'elim as en theois ("among gods") — a direct acknowledgment of the divine council language. At v. 13, the LXX renders vechasdekha as tē dikaiosunē sou ("by Your righteousness"), translating chesed as dikaiosunē ("righteousness") rather than the more common eleos ("mercy") — a significant theological choice that interprets God's covenant loyalty as an act of justice. The LXX also renders neveh qodshekha as hagiasma sou ("Your sanctuary"), interpreting neveh ("dwelling, pasture") as a built sanctuary. At v. 16, the LXX renders am-zu qanita as hon ektēsō ("whom You acquired"), using ktaomai ("to acquire, to possess"). At v. 17, the LXX renders makhon leshivtekha as eis hetoimon katoikētērion sou ("into Your prepared dwelling place"), using katoikētērion ("dwelling place"). At v. 20, the LXX describes Miriam as hē prophētis ("the prophetess"), translating hannevi'ah directly. At v. 23, the LXX renders marim as pikron ("bitter"), and at v. 25, the LXX renders vayyorehu ets as edeixen autō kurios xulon ("the Lord showed him a tree/piece of wood"), using xulon for ets. At v. 26, the LXX renders rofekha as ho iōmenos se ("the one healing you"), using iaomai ("to heal"). Throughout pages 55–56, the Vaticanus text maintains a continuous uncial script without the special shirah layout found in Hebrew manuscripts — the LXX tradition does not preserve the distinctive brick-over-half-brick visual formatting.

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **"He is highly exalted" (v. 1) — ga'oh ga'ah:** Hebrew ki ga'oh ga'ah sus verokhvo ramah vayyam. The construction ga'oh ga'ah uses the infinitive absolute (ga'oh) followed by the perfect verb (ga'ah) from the root g-'-h ("to rise up, to be exalted, to be majestic"). This cognate accusative construction intensifies the verb: He has utterly, completely, magnificently risen up. The root ga'ah is used of the sea's rising (Job 38:11, ge'ut gallav, "the pride/surging of its waves") and of divine majesty (Isa. 12:5, ki ge'ut asah, "for He has done majestically"). There is a deliberate irony in using a word associated with the surging sea to describe God's exaltation over the sea. The verb ramah ("He has hurled, He has thrown") is used of casting an object with force. The combination sus verokhvo ("the horse and its rider") presents the Egyptian military force as a single unit — horse and rider inseparable, hurled together into the sea. This refrain (repeated by Miriam in v. 21) encapsulates the entire Song in a single couplet. LOGOS renders "highly exalted" for ga'oh ga'ah and "hurled" for ramah. The LXX renders endoxōs gar dedoxastai ("for He has been gloriously glorified"), substituting "glory" language for "exaltation" language — a move that connects back to the kavod/glory theme of chapter 14 (ve'ikkavdah, 14:4, 17).

2. **"My strength and my song, Yah" (v. 2) — azzi vezimrat Yah:** Hebrew azzi vezimrat Yah vayehi li lishu'ah zeh Eli ve'anvehu Elohey avi va'aromemenhu. The phrase azzi vezimrat Yah is one of the most debated phrases in the Song. The noun oz ("strength, might") appears here with a first-person singular suffix (azzi, "my strength"). The word zimrat is either from the root z-m-r ("to sing, to make music"), yielding "my song," or from an alternative root z-m-r ("to be strong, to protect"), yielding "my strength/protection" — giving a hendiadys "my strength and my protection is Yah." The abbreviated divine name Yah (shortened from YHWH) appears here for the first time in the Bible. This same phrase (azzi vezimrat Yah) recurs in Isaiah 12:2 and Psalm 118:14, confirming its liturgical importance. The word ve'anvehu (from navah, "to beautify, to adorn, to glorify") or possibly from navah ("to dwell") — "I will make Him a dwelling" — is also debated. The rabbinic tradition (Mekhilta de-Rabbi Ishmael, Shirata 3) interprets it as "I will beautify Him" by performing commandments beautifully. The parallel va'aromemenhu ("and I will exalt Him," Polel of rum, "to lift up") confirms that the verse expresses worship and exaltation. LOGOS renders "my strength and my song" for azzi vezimrat, preserving the traditional musical interpretation and noting the abbreviation Yah. The LXX diverges significantly, rendering boēthos kai skepastēs ("helper and protector"), apparently reading zimrat as "protection" rather than "song."

3. **"The LORD is a man of war" (v. 3) — YHWH ish milchamah:** Hebrew YHWH ish milchamah YHWH shemo. This is the most direct anthropomorphic divine warrior declaration in the Hebrew Bible. The noun ish ("man") applied to YHWH is startling — God is metaphorically portrayed as a human warrior. The construct phrase ish milchamah ("man of war, warrior") characterizes God's essential nature as it has been revealed in the Sea event: the LORD is a fighter, and fighting is His character. The second clause — YHWH shemo ("the LORD is His name") — grounds the warrior identity in the covenant name. The God who fights is not an anonymous deity but the specific, named God of Israel's covenant. The juxtaposition of the anthropomorphic ish milchamah with the sacred name YHWH holds together the personal (warrior) and the transcendent (the Name). This verse becomes the theological foundation for the "Divine Warrior" motif that pervades the Hebrew Bible (cf. Ps. 24:8, YHWH izzuz vegibbor YHWH gibbor milchamah, "the LORD strong and mighty, the LORD mighty in battle"). LOGOS renders "a man of war" literally to preserve the anthropomorphic force. The LXX renders suntribōn polemous ("one who crushes wars"), deliberately avoiding the anthropomorphism and replacing the noun ish with a participial description of divine action — the LXX translator was evidently uncomfortable with calling God a "man."

4. **"The deep waters covered them; they went down into the depths like a stone" (v. 5) — tehomot yekhasuymu yardu bimtsolot kemo-aven:** The noun tehomot (plural of tehom, "the deep, the primordial deep") evokes the tehom of Genesis 1:2 — the deep, chaotic waters over which God's spirit moved before creation. The Sea event is a new act of creation: the same primordial waters that God mastered in Genesis now serve as instruments of judgment. The noun metsolot (plural of metsulah or metsolah, "depths, ocean depths") intensifies the downward motion — the Egyptians do not merely sink but descend into the uttermost depths. The simile kemo-aven ("like a stone") is the first of three sinking similes in the Song: like a stone (v. 5), like lead (v. 10), and by implication like stubble consumed (v. 7). These three materials — stone, lead, and stubble — represent a progression from heavy to heavier, with the combustible stubble standing in contrast as something that is both weightless and destroyable. LOGOS renders "the deep waters" for tehomot and "the depths" for metsolot. The LXX renders pontō ekalupsen autous edisan eis buthon hōsei lithos ("the sea covered them; they sank into the depth like a stone"), using buthos ("depth") and lithos ("stone").

5. **"Who is like You among the gods, O LORD?" (v. 11) — mi kamokha ba'elim YHWH:** Hebrew mi kamokha ba'elim YHWH mi kamokha ne'dar baqqodesh nora tehillot oseh fele. This is the theological apex of the Song — the rhetorical question that declares the incomparability of YHWH. The phrase ba'elim ("among the gods") uses the plural of el ("god, mighty one"), acknowledging the existence of other divine beings while asserting YHWH's absolute supremacy among them. This is not monotheism in the philosophical sense but monolatrous exaltation: whatever other elim exist, none compares to YHWH. The question mi kamokha ("who is like You?") became one of the most important liturgical phrases in Judaism — it is recited daily in the Shacharit (morning) service and gives rise to the name Maccabee (an acronym of Mi Kamokha Ba'elim YHWH, according to rabbinic tradition). The three predicates — ne'dar baqqodesh ("majestic in holiness"), nora tehillot ("fearful in praises" or "awesome in praiseworthy deeds"), and oseh fele ("doing wonders") — move from God's essential nature (holiness) through His effect on worshippers (awe) to His actions in history (wonders). The adjective ne'dar (Niphal participle of adar, "to be majestic, to be glorious") echoes the ne'dari of v. 6 ("majestic in power"), creating a frame: God's right hand is majestic in power (v. 6), and God Himself is majestic in holiness (v. 11). LOGOS renders "Who is like You among the gods" to preserve both the rhetorical force and the divine council language. The LXX renders tis homoios soi en theois kurie ("who is like You among gods, O Lord"), translating directly and preserving the polytheistic reference.

6. **"You have led in Your steadfast love the people whom You have redeemed" (v. 13) — nachita vechasdekha am-zu ga'alta:** Hebrew nachita vechasdekha am-zu ga'alta nehalta be'azzekha el-neveh qodshekha. The verb nachita (Qal perfect of nachah, "to lead, to guide") describes God's role as shepherd-guide. The noun chesed (here vechasdekha, "in/by Your chesed") is one of the most theologically significant words in the Hebrew Bible — it denotes steadfast love, covenant loyalty, faithful kindness, lovingkindness. It combines the ideas of commitment (covenant faithfulness), affection (love), and reliability (steadfastness). No single English word captures its full range. The relative clause am-zu ga'alta ("the people whom You have redeemed") uses the rare demonstrative pronoun zu ("this, whom" — an archaic form found in poetry, cf. Ps. 68:9[10]; Isa. 42:24; Hab. 1:11) and the verb ga'al ("to redeem, to act as kinsman-redeemer"). The ga'al root implies family obligation — the go'el ("redeemer, kinsman-redeemer") is the family member who buys back property, avenges blood, or restores the fortunes of a relative. God's redemption of Israel is thus framed in kinship terms: He is Israel's family redeemer. The destination — neveh qodshekha ("Your holy dwelling") — could refer to Sinai, to the land of Canaan, to Jerusalem, or to the heavenly dwelling. The noun neveh means "pasture, abode, dwelling" and is used of both pastoral settings (Jer. 23:3) and the divine dwelling (2 Sam. 15:25). LOGOS renders "steadfast love" for chesed, "redeemed" for ga'alta, and "holy dwelling" for neveh qodshekha. The LXX renders tē dikaiosunē sou ("by Your righteousness") for vechasdekha — a notable substitution of "righteousness" for "steadfast love" that interprets the Exodus as an act of divine justice rather than covenant kindness.

7. **"You will bring them and plant them on the mountain of Your inheritance" (v. 17) — tevi'emo vitta'emo behar nachalatekha:** Hebrew tevi'emo vitta'emo behar nachalatekha makhon leshivtekha pa'alta YHWH miqdash Adonai konenu yadekha. The verb tevi'emo ("You will bring them," Hiphil of bo') and vitta'emo ("and You will plant them," Qal of nata') form a pair: God is both the one who brings and the one who plants. The agricultural metaphor of "planting" a people is deeply rooted in the Hebrew Bible (cf. 2 Sam. 7:10; Jer. 24:6; Amos 9:15) — Israel is conceived as a plant that God will root in the soil of the promised land. The destination is described in four parallel terms: har nachalatekha ("the mountain of Your inheritance"), makhon leshivtekha ("the fixed place for Your dwelling"), miqdash ("the sanctuary"), and the whole clause konenu yadekha ("Your hands have established"). The word makhon ("established place, fixed place, foundation") is used of the heavenly dwelling (1 Kgs. 8:39, 43, 49) and the temple foundation (Ezra 2:68). The word miqdash ("sanctuary, holy place") anticipates the tabernacle/temple. Whether this verse envisions Sinai, the Jerusalem temple mount, or an idealized divine dwelling is debated — the Song's language is deliberately expansive, encompassing all of Israel's sacred geography in a single poetic vision. The verb konenu ("established," Polel perfect of kun, "to be firm, to be established") with yadekha ("Your hands") attributes the building of the sanctuary to God's own hands — this is divine construction, not human. LOGOS renders "the mountain of Your inheritance" for har nachalatekha and "the sanctuary" for miqdash. The LXX renders ep' orous klēronomias sou ("upon the mountain of Your inheritance") and eis hetoimon katoikētērion sou ("into Your prepared dwelling place"), interpreting makhon as "prepared/ready."

8. **"The LORD will reign forever and ever" (v. 18) — YHWH yimlokh le'olam va'ed:** This verse is the climactic conclusion of the Song. The verb yimlokh ("He will reign," Qal imperfect of malakh) declares God's kingship — the Sea event has demonstrated not merely that God is powerful but that He is king. The phrase le'olam va'ed ("forever and ever," literally "to eternity and further") is the strongest temporal expression available in Hebrew: it stretches beyond olam ("eternity, the hidden future") to add va'ed ("and still, and further"). The declaration of God's eternal kingship is the theological destination of the entire Song: the narrative moves from the specific event (the drowning of Egypt, vv. 1–5) through the general principle (God's warrior-power, vv. 6–10) through the theological question (who is like Him? vv. 11–13) through the eschatological anticipation (the nations tremble, Israel is planted, vv. 14–17) to the universal conclusion (God reigns forever). This verse is recited daily in Jewish liturgy as part of the Shacharit service and is central to the theology of God's kingship (malkhut) in Jewish tradition. LOGOS renders "The LORD will reign forever and ever" literally. The LXX renders kurios basileuōn ton aiōna kai ep' aiōna kai eti ("the Lord reigning for the age and upon the age and still"), preserving the emphatic temporal extension.

9. **"Miriam the prophetess" (v. 20) — Miriam hannevi'ah:** Hebrew vattiqqach Miriam hannevi'ah achot Aharon et-hatof beyadah vattetse'na khol-hannashim achareha betuppim uvimcholot. Miriam is here given the title hannevi'ah ("the prophetess"), making her the first woman in the Torah to receive this designation. The title navi'/nevi'ah ("prophet/prophetess") denotes one who speaks on behalf of God — a mediator of divine communication. Miriam is identified as achot Aharon ("the sister of Aaron") rather than "the sister of Moses," which has generated extensive discussion. Some scholars suggest this reflects an older tradition in which Miriam's connection to Aaron was more prominent, or that Moses is omitted because his role as Song-leader has just been described. The instrument hatof ("the timbrel, the hand drum") is a frame drum beaten by hand, associated with women's celebration in the ancient Near East (cf. Judg. 11:34; 1 Sam. 18:6; Ps. 68:26[25]). The verb vattetse'na ("and they went out," Qal imperfect 3fp of yatsa') describes the women going out in procession — a public, visible, communal act of celebration. The combination betuppim uvimcholot ("with timbrels and with dances") pairs percussion with bodily movement: Israel's worship is not merely vocal but physical. LOGOS renders "the prophetess" for hannevi'ah and "the sister of Aaron" as given. The LXX renders hē prophētis ("the prophetess") and hē adelphē Aarōn ("the sister of Aaron").

10. **"And Miriam answered them" (v. 21) — vatta'an lahem Miriam:** The verb vatta'an ("and she answered," Qal imperfect 3fs of anah) indicates a responsive or antiphonal performance: Miriam "answers" or "responds to" the men's Song by leading the women in the refrain. The pronoun lahem ("to them") is masculine plural, suggesting Miriam is responding to Moses and the men of Israel. This antiphonal structure — a lead singer performing the full Song, with a chorus responding with the refrain — is characteristic of ancient Near Eastern victory celebrations and is attested elsewhere in the Hebrew Bible (cf. 1 Sam. 18:7, where women sing responsively). The content of Miriam's response is identical to v. 1b: shiru laYHWH ki ga'oh ga'ah sus verokhvo ramah vayyam ("Sing to the LORD, for He is highly exalted; the horse and its rider He has hurled into the sea"), with the sole change from ashirah ("I will sing," 1cs) to shiru ("Sing!" 2mp imperative). This shift from first-person declaration to second-person command transforms the Song from personal praise to communal exhortation. LOGOS renders "answered" for vatta'an. The LXX renders exērchen gar autois Mariam ("for Miriam led them"), using exarchō ("to lead, to begin"), interpreting anah as "to lead [a song]" rather than "to answer."

11. **"And the LORD showed him a tree" (v. 25) — vayyorehu YHWH ets:** Hebrew vayyits'aq el-YHWH vayyorehu YHWH ets vayyashleikh el-hammayim vayyimtequ hammayim. The verb vayyorehu (Hiphil of yarah, "to teach, to instruct, to show, to point out") is the same root that gives the noun torah ("instruction, teaching, law"). God "torah-ed" Moses — He showed/instructed him. The object is ets ("tree, wood, piece of wood") — the text does not specify what kind of tree. The casting of the tree into the bitter waters and their transformation into sweet water is the narrative action, but the theological weight falls on the clause that follows: sham sam lo choq umishpat vesham nissahu ("there He made for them a statute and an ordinance, and there He tested them"). The Marah event is simultaneously a miracle (water transformation), a legislative moment (statute and ordinance), and a test (nissahu, Piel of nasah, "to test, to try, to prove"). The juxtaposition of healing and law-giving anticipates Sinai: obedience to divine instruction is linked to divine provision. LOGOS renders "showed" for vayyorehu and "tree" for ets. The LXX renders edeixen autō kurios xulon ("the Lord showed him a tree/piece of wood"), using deiknumi ("to show, to point out") and xulon ("wood, tree").

12. **"For I am the LORD your healer" (v. 26) — ki ani YHWH rofekha:** Hebrew kol-hammachalah asher-samti beMitsrayim lo-asim alekha ki ani YHWH rofekha. The participle rofekha (Qal active participle of rafa', "to heal, to cure, to restore" + 2ms suffix, "your healer") is a divine self-designation — God names Himself as Israel's physician. The root rafa' is used of physical healing (Gen. 20:17; 2 Kgs. 20:5), national restoration (2 Chr. 7:14, "I will heal their land"), and spiritual renewal (Ps. 103:3, "who heals all your diseases"). Here, the healing metaphor encompasses all of these: God heals the bitter waters (physical), prevents the diseases of Egypt (medical), and establishes the covenant framework within which healing operates (spiritual). The conditional structure — im shamo'a tishma ... kol-hammachalah ... lo-asim — makes healing contingent on obedience: listening (shamo'a tishma, "listening you shall listen," another infinitive absolute construction for emphasis), doing right (hayyashar be'enav, "what is right in His eyes"), giving ear (ha'azanta, Hiphil of azan, "to listen with the ear"), and keeping (shamarta, "you shall keep/guard"). The divine self-revelation ki ani YHWH rofekha ("for I am the LORD your healer") parallels the formula ki ani YHWH ("for I am the LORD") that pervades Leviticus and Ezekiel. LOGOS renders "your healer" for rofekha. The LXX renders ego eimi kurios ho iōmenos se ("I am the Lord the one healing you"), using the participle of iaomai ("to heal").

### Theological Note: The Song of the Sea as Theological Poetry

Exodus 15 is the first major poem in the Torah and one of the oldest compositions in the Hebrew Bible. Linguistic analysis of the Song (vv. 1–18) reveals archaic Hebrew features: the use of the relative pronoun zu (v. 13) instead of the standard asher, the archaic verbal suffix -mo (yokhlemo, v. 7; timla'emo, v. 9; tivla'emo, v. 12; kissamo, v. 10; torishemo, v. 9; tevi'emo, vitta'emo, v. 17), the shortened forms of prepositions, and distinctive vocabulary. These features suggest that the Song preserves very early poetic tradition, possibly predating the prose narrative that surrounds it.

The Song's structure moves through five stanzas of increasing theological scope. The first stanza (vv. 1–5) celebrates the specific event: the drowning of Egypt's army. The second stanza (vv. 6–10) generalizes from the event to God's warrior-power, with the climactic destruction effected by divine breath (ruach appekha, v. 8) and divine wind (beruchakha, v. 10). The third stanza (vv. 11–13) pivots from celebration to theology: the event prompts the question of incomparability (mi kamokha) and the recognition of God's chesed ("steadfast love") as the motive for redemption. The fourth stanza (vv. 14–16a) extends the terror of the Sea event to the nations that lie between Egypt and Canaan — Philistia, Edom, Moab, and Canaan itself. The fifth stanza (vv. 16b–18) projects the Sea event forward to the entry into the land and the establishment of the sanctuary, culminating in the declaration of God's eternal kingship.

This forward-looking trajectory — from the Sea to the sanctuary to the eternal reign — transforms the Song from a victory hymn into a prophetic declaration. The Song does not merely celebrate what has happened; it announces what will happen. The planting on the mountain (v. 17), the establishment of the sanctuary (v. 17), and the eternal reign (v. 18) are presented as already accomplished in poetic vision, even though they remain future in narrative time. This prophetic compression of time — in which the past event guarantees the future fulfillment — is characteristic of Hebrew poetry and becomes a foundation for Israel's eschatological hope.

The narrative section (vv. 19–27) that follows the Song creates a sharp theological contrast. The Song's exalted poetry gives way immediately to the prose of complaint. The same people who sang "Who is like You?" (v. 11) now murmur "What shall we drink?" (v. 24). The same God who hurled horse and rider into the sea (v. 1) now shows Moses a tree to sweeten water (v. 25). The movement from cosmic triumph to domestic provision — from the Sea of Reeds to the bitter pool at Marah — is the defining pattern of the wilderness narrative: God's greatest acts of deliverance are followed immediately by Israel's inability to trust the deliverer.

The Marah episode (vv. 22–26) introduces the first post-exodus legislation and the first post-exodus test. The phrase sham sam lo choq umishpat vesham nissahu ("there He made for them a statute and an ordinance, and there He tested them," v. 25) ties obedience to provision: God heals the water, then establishes the framework of law, then tests whether Israel will live within that framework. The climactic self-revelation — ki ani YHWH rofekha ("for I am the LORD your healer," v. 26) — names God not as warrior (ish milchamah, v. 3) but as physician. The God who destroys also heals. The God who judges Egypt also preserves Israel from Egypt's diseases. The conditional structure ("if you will diligently listen... I will not put upon you") establishes the covenantal pattern that will govern the Sinai legislation: divine blessing is contingent on human obedience, and divine healing operates within the framework of divine instruction.

The arrival at Elim (v. 27) — with its twelve springs and seventy palms — provides a moment of rest and abundance. The twelve springs correspond to the twelve tribes; the seventy palms correspond to the seventy elders (cf. Num. 11:16) or to the seventy descendants of Jacob who entered Egypt (Gen. 46:27). The numbers signal completeness and sufficiency: God provides not merely enough but a full and ordered abundance. After the bitterness of Marah, Elim is the oasis of grace — the proof that the LORD who heals (rofekha) also provides.

The shirah layout of the Song in the Leningrad Codex — the "brick-over-half-brick" pattern — is itself a theological statement. The visual alternation of text and space on the page has been interpreted by Jewish tradition as representing the walls of water with the open path between them. The Song is not merely read; it is seen. The physical appearance of the text on the page reenacts the miracle it describes. This visual dimension of Scripture — in which the form of the writing participates in the meaning of the words — is unique to the Song of the Sea and the Song of Moses (Deut. 32), marking these two poems as the supreme liturgical compositions of the Torah.

---

*Note on the LXX (Vaticanus):* The Vaticanus text of Exodus 15 (pages 55–56) preserves the LXX version with several notable divergences from the MT. The most significant is at v. 2, where the LXX renders azzi vezimrat Yah as boēthos kai skepastēs ("helper and protector"), reading zimrat not as "song" but as a derivative of a root meaning "protection/strength," and suppressing the abbreviated divine name Yah entirely. At v. 3, the LXX's avoidance of the anthropomorphic ish milchamah ("man of war") in favor of suntribōn polemous ("one who crushes wars") reflects the Hellenistic Jewish tendency to soften anthropomorphisms in the divine portrayal — a pattern consistent throughout the LXX. At v. 8, the LXX's rendering of kemo-ned ("like a heap") as hōsei teichos ("like a wall") harmonizes the Song's language with the prose account in 14:22, 29, where the waters are described as chomah ("a wall"). At v. 13, the LXX's substitution of dikaiosunē ("righteousness") for chesed ("steadfast love") is theologically significant: it reframes the Exodus as an act of divine justice — God redeems Israel because it is righteous/just to do so — rather than an act of covenant loyalty. This reflects a broader LXX tendency to interpret chesed through the lens of Greek ethical categories. At v. 16, the LXX renders am-zu qanita as laon touton hon ektēsō ("this people whom You acquired"), using the standard relative pronoun rather than preserving the archaic zu. At v. 17, the LXX expands the sanctuary reference, rendering miqdash as hagiasma ("sanctuary, consecrated place") and adding kurie ("O Lord") in a way that makes the divine kingship theme more explicit. At v. 20, the LXX identifies Miriam as hē prophētis hē adelphē Aarōn ("the prophetess, the sister of Aaron"), faithfully translating both titles. At v. 25, the LXX renders vayyorehu as edeixen autō ("He showed him"), interpreting the Hiphil of yarah as "to show/point out" rather than "to instruct/teach." At v. 26, the LXX's ho iōmenos se ("the one healing you") uses iaomai, the standard Greek medical term for healing, connecting the divine epithet to the Hellenistic medical tradition. Throughout pages 55–56, the Vaticanus text does not reproduce the shirah layout of the Hebrew tradition — the Song is written in the same continuous uncial format as the surrounding prose, without the visual structuring that marks the Hebrew manuscripts. This difference highlights the fact that the shirah layout is a feature of the Hebrew scribal tradition, not of the textual content itself — the same words, differently arranged, carry different visual and liturgical weight.

--- LOGOS
